does: This paper introduces DeltaRubric, a generative approach for building multimodal reward models that evaluate responses from MLLMs by decomposing evaluation into two stages. First, the model acts as a Disagreement Planner, generating an instance-specific verification checklist that identifies where two responses likely differ based on visual and factual details. Second, it transitions to a Checklist Verifier that systematically checks these criteria against the image and question to produce a grounded preference judgment. This two-step process addresses the tendency of single-pass evaluators to rely on language priors rather than performing genuine visual verification, yielding more reliable rewards for MLLM alignment.
connects: This work builds on recent efforts to improve reward modeling for language model alignment, particularly extending rubric-based evaluation approaches that have successfully reduced bias in text-only settings to the multimodal domain. It responds to documented failures of existing single-step multimodal evaluators that exploit shallow linguistic patterns rather than engaging in detailed visual reasoning. The approach combines insights from planning-based reasoning and verification-based methods, drawing on the broader literature of process-based reward models and self-improvement through structured reasoning in large language models.
enables: DeltaRubric opens pathways for more robust evaluation systems in multimodal AI alignment by demonstrating how to leverage generative models themselves to construct fine-grained, dynamic evaluation criteria. This methodology could extend to other multimodal tasks requiring detailed visual understanding and enable more reliable preference data collection for training aligned MLLMs at scale. The plan-and-verify framework also suggests future work on iterative refinement of evaluation processes, compositional verification for complex visual reasoning, and applications to broader domains like video understanding or embodied AI evaluation where instance-specific context heavily influences correctness.
